findings.title: HIGH_STAKES tier lacks the output-length cap that BULK tier enforces (asymmetric runaway-generation guard)
findings.description: The two reasoning tiers are parallel mechanisms that both must bound a single LLM call's cost/duration. BULK got a hard generation cap: `_bulk_complete` always sets `num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT` (line 99, 368), and the docstring at lines 95-99 explicitly argues this is 'the real guard' because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a backend call already blocked in generation. HIGH_STAKES (`_high_stakes_complete`, lines 414-503) builds `ClaudeAgentOptions` with NO max-output-tokens equivalent — there is no `max_tokens`/output bound passed at all. The result is asymmetric: a runaway BULK generation is bounded by tokens AND wall-clock, but a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation is bounded only by `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` wall-clock. For the tier explicitly described as 'gated, traced' and the one that consumes the fixed-cost subscription budget, the cheaper/safer of the two guards (a bounded output) was applied only to the local tier. The same reasoning the BULK docstring uses ('the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in the backend') applies to the subscription subprocess.
findings.poc: Set ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT=2048 (default). A BULK call cannot emit more than 2048 tokens. The parallel HIGH_STAKES call has no analogous bound: a prompt that elicits a very long completion runs until HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default), consuming the full subscription turn and 300s of a bounded-pool worker slot, where BULK would have stopped at 2048 tokens well inside its window. grep for `num_predict` shows it only in `_bulk_complete`; grep for `max_tokens` in seam.py returns nothing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parallel HIGH_STAKES output cap (e.g. ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MAX_TOKENS) and pass it to ClaudeAgentOptions (or the SDK's max-output-tokens option), mirroring `num_predict`. If the Agent SDK genuinely cannot bound output, document that asymmetry explicitly in the HIGH_STAKES docstring the way BULK's is documented, so the gap is intentional-and-recorded rather than silent.

findings.title: discover_and_confirm lacks the on_step provenance hook that adjudicate_with_baseline has (asymmetric audit-trail wiring)
findings.description: `adjudicate` / `adjudicate_with_baseline` and `discover_and_confirm` are the two parallel firewall runners (the discovery module docstring even calls itself 'The mirror of assay_engine.methodology.adjudication'). They were hardened asymmetrically for per-step provenance. `adjudicate_with_baseline` takes an `on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None` parameter (adjudication.py:193, 264-267) so a caller (e.g. the pipeline at pipeline.py:641-668) can record each pre-registered hypothesis and its verdict to the hash-chained provenance trail *as each happens* (#93 in the code comment). `discover_and_confirm` (discovery.py:69-165) has NO such hook: a study driving the standalone discovery runner cannot capture per-hypothesis/per-verdict provenance in order; it gets only the returned list, forcing a post-hoc batch record that loses the 'recorded before the next step runs' GOVERNANCE §3 ordering guarantee that the adjudication path preserves. One mirror got the governance seam; the other did not.
findings.poc: Call adjudicate_with_baseline(..., on_step=lambda h,v: trail.record(...)) — each verdict lands in the trail in confirmation order. There is no equivalent for discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=..., authority=...): the function signature accepts no on_step, so a standalone discovery study can only record verdicts after the whole loop returns (or by re-implementing the loop), defeating the append-before-next-step ordering the adjudication twin enforces.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None = None` parameter to discover_and_confirm and invoke it after each verdict is validated (right after the misattribution check at discovery.py:164), mirroring adjudication.py:264-267, so both firewall runners expose the same per-step provenance seam.

findings.title: Confirm step double-dip leak check (assert_confirm_only) enforced by confirm_unit_level but not by the discovery runners
findings.description: `DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime guard that the confirm step touched ONLY held-out ids (not empty, no discovery-id leak, none outside the confirm partition). `confirm_unit_level` calls it on the caller-supplied `evaluated_ids` (confirm.py:188) — the standalone confirm primitive verifies which ids were actually evaluated. The two composing runners that ALSO confirm — `discover_and_confirm` (discovery.py) and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block (pipeline.py:558-576) — never call `assert_confirm_only`. They guard only that the held-out *partition* is non-empty (discovery.py:120, pipeline.py:526) and subset the corpus to confirm_ids, but the study-supplied `confirm`/`confirm_held_out` callable is handed the held-out corpus and is trusted to evaluate within it: nothing checks the ids it actually scored. So a confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units (e.g. via attributes/metadata references the subset_corpus docstring admits it cannot police) is caught by the standalone primitive but NOT by either runner. The same Firewall-B leak check exists on one parallel mechanism and is absent on the other two.
findings.poc: Build a confirm_held_out that ignores its held_out arg and evaluates discovery-partition units. confirm_unit_level(hyp, split=split, evaluated_ids=<discovery ids>, ...) raises FirewallViolation 'touched N discovery id(s) — double-dipping'. The same logical leak through discover_and_confirm(... confirm=that_callable ...) or run_study completes cleanly because neither runner asks the confirmer which ids it evaluated, so assert_confirm_only is never exercised.
findings.suggested_fix: Have the discovery runners obtain (or require) the set of ids the confirmer evaluated and pass it through split.assert_confirm_only before accepting the verdict — e.g. change HeldOutConfirmer to return evaluated ids alongside the verdict, or have the runner pass split to the confirmer and assert. At minimum, document in both runners that the confirm-time double-dip check is delegated to the study (as adjudication documents its frame-isolation caveat), so the asymmetry with confirm_unit_level is explicit.

findings.title: Direction shape-validation present in confirm_unit_level but the keyed-direction resolution differs from confirm_whole_corpus (asymmetric tail-binding)
findings.description: confirm_whole_corpus and confirm_unit_level are the two parallel confirmatory mechanisms. They handle the pre-registered `predicted_direction` asymmetrically in a way that is documented but worth flagging as a cross-tier divergence in guard strength. confirm_whole_corpus routes the locked direction through `_resolve_direction` (confirm.py:254-289), which REFUSES a directionless-but-locked hypothesis (None → HARKing guard, #G-001) and rejects any tail not in {greater,less} that would silently flip the verdict (#128). confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:180-186) only *shape-validates* a non-None predicted_direction and otherwise lets the caller-supplied `direction_supports_claim` flag decide — a locked hypothesis with predicted_direction=None passes unit-level confirmation with the tail effectively chosen post-hoc by the caller, exactly the HARKing case _resolve_direction refuses on the whole-corpus side. The asymmetry is intentional (documented at confirm.py:163-169) but the unit-level path lacks the directionless-lock refusal its twin enforces, so a study can pick the supporting/contradicting polarity after seeing held-out data on the unit-level path while the whole-corpus path forbids it.
findings.poc: Lock a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis with predicted_direction=None. confirm_unit_level(..., direction_supports_claim=True) returns 'supported'; flip the flag and the SAME locked hypothesis returns 'contradicted'. The whole-corpus twin refuses this outright: _resolve_direction raises 'predicted_direction was not pre-registered ... refused (#G-001)'. The polarity is caller-chosen at confirm time on one mechanism and locked-or-refused on the other.
findings.suggested_fix: If unit-level studies are expected to bind the tail at lock time, add a symmetric refusal: when a unit-level hypothesis is locked, require predicted_direction to be non-None and (optionally) require the study to derive direction_supports_claim from it. If the asymmetry must stand, strengthen the confirm_unit_level docstring's caveat into an explicit runtime warning when direction_supports_claim is supplied for a directionless-locked hypothesis, mirroring _gate_preregistration's authority=None warning.

findings.title: Gate-decision provenance recording: graph gate node warns-but-allows a missing recorder while run_study's gate path always records (asymmetric audit-trail guarantee)
findings.description: There are two parallel governance-gate execution paths and they were hardened asymmetrically with respect to the hash-chained provenance trail. In run_study, the gate handler ALWAYS records to the trail before enforcing (pipeline.py:326-342, run_gate): every gate decision is unconditionally appended to the tamper-evident chain. The LangGraph graph path (make_gate_node, gatenode.py:90-161) makes the trail recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it merely emits a warning (lines 114-120) and records the decision only into (non-tamper-evident) LangGraph state. The docstring itself (lines 109-112) calls this 'a GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected→approved is undetectable by verify_records. So the same governance concern (gate decisions must be in the hash-chained trail) is structurally guaranteed on the run_study path and merely warned-about on the graph path. The two parallel mechanisms enforce GOVERNANCE §3 at different strengths.
findings.poc: make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (only a warning). Operator approvals/rejections then live solely in LangGraph checkpoint state; verify_records on the provenance trail cannot detect a checkpoint tamper that flips the decision. The equivalent run_study path has no such opt-out — run_gate always calls trail.record before raising/continuing, so every decision is in the chain.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the recorder mandatory on make_gate_node (raise instead of warn when recorder is None), matching run_study's unconditional trail.record — or, if a recorder-less graph node must remain supported, document and gate it behind an explicit opt-out flag (e.g. allow_unrecorded=True) so shipping the audit-gap configuration is a conscious choice rather than the default, mirroring how run_study forces gate_handler to be passed explicitly.

findings.title: Provenance-artifact temp file leaked on every tracked run (delete=False, never unlinked)
findings.description: In run_study's best-effort _log_trail callback, the hash-chained provenance trail is serialized to a NamedTemporaryFile opened with delete=False, then handed to tracker.log_artifact(rid, path). The temp file is never unlinked afterward. MLflow's log_artifact copies the file into the artifact store but does not remove the source, so each successful study run that has a tracker+run_id leaves one orphaned <tmp>_provenance.json in the system temp directory. Over a long-running campaign / many studies this accumulates unbounded disk under TMPDIR. The leak also persists if log_artifact raises, because _log_trail runs inside track(), which swallows the exception (recording a tracking_error) but the file is already created and orphaned. This is the one place in the codebase that copies the in-memory provenance (which can contain study summaries / payloads) to a world-readable default-mode temp path and leaves it there — a minor data-at-rest hygiene issue on top of the disk leak. Contrast with versioning.py:74 which correctly uses mkstemp + try/finally tmp.unlink(missing_ok=True).
findings.poc: Run run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) to completion N times; observe N orphaned files matching $TMPDIR/tmp*_provenance.json that are never cleaned. Each run grows temp-dir usage by the serialized trail size; nothing ever removes them (no atexit, no unlink).
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the artifact upload in try/finally and unlink the temp file after log_artifact returns (or fails): create the path, write+flush+close, then `try: t.log_artifact(rid, path) finally: os.unlink(path)` with missing_ok semantics. Prefer writing into a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() context so both the file and dir are cleaned deterministically, mirroring versioning.py's mkstemp+try/finally pattern. Optionally set restrictive perms (0o600) since the file holds provenance payloads.

findings.title: Operator-username PII not redacted from transcripts when home-dir name is short (<4 chars) or appears in dash-delimited project slug
findings.description: _scrub redacts the operator's username two ways: the path rule `(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s"']+` (requires literal '/Users/[REDACTED]') and _USERNAME_RE derived from Path.home().name. The Claude project-dir slug that DEFAULT_SRC points at is dash-delimited (e.g. '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc'); the path rule does NOT match it (no slashes), so the only thing masking the username inside slugs, git-author strings, and prose is _USERNAME_RE. But _username_pattern() returns None for any home-dir name shorter than 4 chars (to avoid over-redacting common words). For operators whose login is <4 chars (e.g. 'bob','amy','ci', 'root' is also skipped), the username then leaks verbatim into committed transcripts via the slug and any prose mention — defeating the stated #F-024/#G-007 PII-scrubbing goal for that class of operator. The capture script's own docstring claims credential-shaped secrets AND operator PII are redacted, so this is an undisclosed completeness gap rather than documented honest scope.
findings.poc: Set HOME so Path.home().name='bob'; create $SRC=.../-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc/<id>.jsonl containing the slug path and an author line 'bob'. Run capture_transcripts (or commit via the pre-commit hook). The committed transcripts/sessions/<id>.jsonl still contains 'bob' and '-Users-[REDACTED]-...' unredacted because _USERNAME_RE is None (len<4) and the '/Users/' path rule never matches the dash slug.
findings.suggested_fix: Always derive the slug form of the home path (replace os.sep with '-') and redact it explicitly regardless of username length; and lower/remove the len<4 skip in favor of a word-boundary-anchored token redaction so short usernames are still masked inside slugs and author strings without clobbering arbitrary common words. At minimum, document in the docstring + transcripts/README that usernames <4 chars are NOT auto-redacted so reviewers know to scrub before publishing.

findings.title: Module-global reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor cannot recover after interpreter-level shutdown; saturation guard can wedge if a worker thread dies
findings.description: The reasoning seam uses a single process-global ThreadPoolExecutor (_pool) created at import and shut down via atexit(wait=False). _submit_bounded increments _inflight before submit and relies on add_done_callback(_release) to decrement it when the future completes. The accounting is carefully guarded for KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit during submit/callback attach, and for pool-shutdown (RuntimeError -> PermanentReasoningError with decrement). However, there is no recovery path if a pool worker THREAD itself dies abnormally without the future ever completing (e.g. a C-level crash in a backend that takes down the worker but not the process, or _pool internal state corruption): the done-callback never fires, _inflight is never decremented, and after _POOL_WORKERS such events every subsequent call raises RateLimitError('pool saturated') permanently — bricking all reasoning for the process lifetime with no self-heal (the checkpoint pool, by contrast, is explicitly self-healing). This is a latent availability/leak risk on the exception path that the in-flight accounting does not cover; future.result() timeouts are handled (slot intentionally held), but a vanished future is not.
findings.poc: Simulate: monkeypatch _pool.submit to return a Future that is never resolved (worker 'died'); call _with_timeout 8 times -> each times out (ReasoningError) but the futures never complete, so _release never runs. The 9th _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError unconditionally and stays that way forever; is_available()/run() for BULK/HIGH_STAKES are effectively dead with no recovery short of process restart.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a self-heal/watchdog: track each in-flight future with its submission time and, in _submit_bounded's saturation branch, reap futures that are done-but-unreleased or that have exceeded a hard ceiling (per-call timeout + grace) by force-calling _release on them before refusing. Alternatively decrement _inflight in a finally around future.result() in _with_timeout for the timeout case combined with a per-future released flag (already present) so a vanished worker cannot permanently consume a slot. Document that wait=False atexit means in-flight backend calls may be abandoned at exit.

findings.title: alpha and stability_threshold are post-hoc free parameters, not pre-registered — HARKing on the decision threshold survives the firewall
findings.description: The pre-registration digest (canonical_hypothesis_digest, preregistration.py:109-119) cryptographically binds statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params, and audit pass 4 (#G-001) closed direction-after-the-fact HARKing. But the two parameters that actually decide the verdict — the significance level `alpha` and the `stability_threshold` — are NOT fields on Hypothesis, are NOT in the digest, and are supplied as plain runtime arguments to verdict_from_pvalue / confirm_whole_corpus / confirm_unit_level at confirmation time. Nothing in the engine reads `hypothesis.params` or `hypothesis.decision_rule` to constrain the confirm-time alpha/threshold. `verdict_from_pvalue` even *constructs* the recorded decision_rule string from the runtime alpha (`rule = f"...alpha={alpha}..."`, line 131) instead of comparing it against the locked `decision_rule`. So an analyst (or a study confirmer) who has already seen the baseline/null distribution can choose alpha=0.05 vs 0.10, or stability_threshold=0.9 vs 0.6, to flip indeterminate↔supported — exactly the post-hoc-threshold HARKing the pre-registration firewall claims to prevent. The locked `decision_rule` text (which a study might write 'alpha=0.01' into) is decorative: the engine never enforces it. This defeats the methodological guarantee that 'the decision rule is committed before confirmation' for its most decision-bearing numbers.
findings.poc: 1) Build/lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater' (alpha is NOT a field, so nothing about the threshold is locked). 2) After confirmation data is in hand, observe the null distribution and the observed statistic. 3) confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=X, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=R, stability_threshold=0.9, authority=auth) → indeterminate ('does not beat null at alpha'). 4) Re-run the SAME locked hypothesis (require_preregistered still passes — content unchanged) with alpha=0.10, stability_threshold=0.6 → supported. The verdict was changed purely by post-hoc threshold choice; require_preregistered raised nothing because alpha/stability_threshold are outside the digest. Same applies to confirm_unit_level's `alpha`.
findings.suggested_fix: Make alpha and stability_threshold (and the test sidedness already covered) part of the pre-registered, content-bound contract. Concretely: add `alpha` (and optional `stability_threshold`) as typed fields on Hypothesis, include them in canonical_hypothesis_digest's payload, and have confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level READ them from the locked hypothesis instead of accepting them as caller arguments (or, if kept as arguments, require they equal the locked values and raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch — mirroring _resolve_direction's cross-check). Until then the runners (adjudication/discovery/pipeline) cannot certify the threshold was committed before the data was seen.

findings.title: `powered` is a trusted, unbound caller flag that gates indeterminate↔decisive after the data is seen
findings.description: verdict_from_pvalue takes `powered: bool` from the caller and routes `not powered → indeterminate` (line 137-138). `powered` is neither a Hypothesis field nor part of the pre-registration digest, and the engine performs no power calculation. A study confirmer therefore decides AFTER seeing the held-out result whether the test was 'powered enough' to be decisive. Combined with M-1, this is a second post-hoc lever: an unfavorable significant-but-against-the-claim result can be neutralized to indeterminate by asserting powered=False, or a favorable result promoted to decisive by asserting powered=True. The docstring frames `powered` as a method-capability fact, but nothing binds it to a pre-stated power/sample-size criterion.
findings.poc: For one locked unit-level hypothesis with a fixed held-out statistic/p_value, call confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True, ...) → supported/contradicted; call again with powered=False → indeterminate. Both pass the identical pre-registration check; the decisiveness was chosen post-hoc.
findings.suggested_fix: Require the power/decisiveness criterion (e.g. minimum n, minimum detectable effect) to be pre-registered in the hypothesis (params bound in the digest) and have the engine derive `powered` from the held-out sample it actually evaluated against that locked criterion, rather than accepting an opaque boolean. At minimum, record in evidence the locked power criterion and assert the supplied `powered` is consistent with it.

findings.title: Locked `test_name` is bound in the digest but never enforced against the test actually executed (test-choice HARKing at unit level)
findings.description: test_name is included in canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:114), so it cannot be swapped after locking — but `grep` shows test_name is read NOWHERE except when building the digest. No confirmer or runner checks that the test the study actually ran matches the pre-registered test_name. On the unit-level path this matters most: confirm_unit_level receives only an opaque `statistic`/`p_value`/`direction_supports_claim` (the engine 'cannot itself infer the tail', lines 163-169), and verdict_from_pvalue records a hard-coded test_description='held-out unit-level test' (line 196) rather than the locked test_name. So a study can pre-register test_name='mann_whitney_u', then, after seeing the held-out data, run whichever test gives the desired p_value and feed it in — the pre-registration check still passes because the content (including the now-meaningless test_name) is unchanged. The methodology docstrings claim the locked content governs the verdict; for the test choice it does not.
findings.poc: Lock a unit-level hypothesis with test_name='mann_whitney_u'. After seeing the held-out partition, compute p_value with a t-test (which happens to clear alpha) and call confirm_unit_level(h, ..., p_value=that_t_test_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth) → supported. require_preregistered raises nothing; the executed test silently differs from the locked one.
findings.suggested_fix: Have the engine-side confirmer pass the test identity it ran and assert it equals hypothesis.test_name (raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch), and propagate test_name into verdict_from_pvalue's test_description so the recorded decision_rule reflects the locked test rather than a generic label. Where the engine cannot see the test (fully study-owned confirmers), document explicitly that test-choice integrity is an un-enforced caller obligation so the docstrings stop over-claiming.

findings.title: G-004 in-process flaky guard: unfreeze(frozenset) order test mis-passes ~30% of runs against the buggy code
findings.description: test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (lines 43-48) asserts unfreeze(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) == [1,2,'a','b'] while running at the AMBIENT PYTHONHASHSEED (pytest does not pin it). The correct implementation sorts (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:158-161), so the test passes today. But its stated job is to be a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration (e.g. `list(value)`). As such a guard it is probabilistic: I enumerated 200 seeds and raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} equals the stable order [1,2,'a','b'] in 60/200 = 30% of seeds. So on the buggy raw-iteration code this test would still PASS ~30% of the time, depending only on the process hash seed. The test's own comment at lines 52-53 admits 'the in-process assertion above can pass ~1/3 of the time even on the buggy raw-iteration code.' Secondary defect: lines 47 and 48 use {1,'a',2,'b'} and {'b',2,'a',1}, which are the SAME set object — identical iteration order in any given process — so line 48 adds zero additional coverage and gives a false impression of testing two independent orderings.
findings.poc:
# enumerate seeds: raw iteration == stable order 30% of the time
for s in range(200):
    out = subprocess.run([sys.executable,'-c',"print(list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})))"],
                         env={'PYTHONHASHSEED':str(s),'PATH':''},capture_output=True,text=True).stdout.strip()
    # out == "[1, 2, 'a', 'b']" for seeds 4,8,10,12,13,14,18,23,29,33,35,37,39,... (60/200)
# => if unfreeze() reverts to `return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]`, this test passes whenever
#    the ambient PYTHONHASHSEED lands on one of those ~30% of seeds, masking the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Treat the in-process assertion as documentation only, not a guard; rely on the subprocess test (test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess) for discrimination. If keeping the in-process check, parametrize it to run under several pinned PYTHONHASHSEEDs that are known to differ from the stable order (re-run in subprocess), and replace the duplicate {'b',2,'a',1} set on line 48 with a genuinely different input (e.g. a 3-type mix like frozenset({1,'a',2.5,'b'})) so the two assertions actually cover distinct cases.

findings.title: Subprocess seed-pinned guard rests on a hardcoded, version-dependent iteration order that is already wrong on Python 3.14
findings.description: test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 and the comment at lines 65-66 asserts 'raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} is ['b',1,2,'a'] (verified)'. On the repo's interpreter (Python 3.14, .venv) the raw order at seed=2 is actually [1, 2, 'b', 'a'], not ['b',1,2,'a'] — the documented 'verified' fact is stale/wrong. The test's discriminating power depends on the chosen seed producing a raw order that DIFFERS from the stable [1,2,'a','b']; seed=2 happens to still differ on 3.14 (last two elements swapped), so the guard works by luck today, but the seed was empirically chosen against a specific CPython hash-randomization implementation. A future CPython that changes small-set hashing/iteration could make seed=2 coincide with the stable order (as 30% of seeds already do), silently turning this into a non-discriminating test that passes on the reverted buggy code. The guard is correct in intent but its single hardcoded seed is a hidden version dependency.
findings.poc:
$ PYTHONHASHSEED=2 .venv/bin/python -c "print(list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})))"
[1, 2, 'b', 'a']   # NOT ['b', 1, 2, 'a'] as the comment claims
# seeds where raw == stable [1,2,'a','b'] (guard would be vacuous if one were chosen): 4,8,10,12,13,14,18,23,29,33,35,37,39,...
findings.suggested_fix: Do not depend on one hardcoded seed. Make the subprocess assert the property directly: in the child, compute raw=list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) and stable=unfreeze(...); skip/retry seeds where raw==stable and require that for at least one seed where raw!=stable the unfreeze output still equals [1,2,'a','b']. Or loop over a handful of seeds (e.g. 0..9) and assert unfreeze() returns the stable order for every one, so no single seed's coincidence can mask a revert. Correct the stale 'verified' comment.

findings.title: License gate silently passes UNKNOWN / empty / missing-license dependencies
findings.description: The license gate denies a fixed family of strong-copyleft tokens but treats any package whose License field is 'UNKNOWN', empty, or absent as ALLOWED (the deny loop only matches known tokens; a missing field yields ''). pip-licenses frequently reports UNKNOWN for packages that ship the license only in a classifier/LICENSE file or that use a non-standard metadata field. A genuinely GPL/AGPL/SSPL dependency whose metadata pip-licenses cannot parse therefore bypasses the Apache-2.0 compatibility gate entirely — the exact supply-chain blind spot the gate exists to close. Verified empirically: feeding the gate three packages with License 'UNKNOWN', '' and a missing key returns exit code 0 ('license gate OK').
findings.poc:
python3 - <<'PY'
import json, importlib.util
json.dump([{'Name':'evil','Version':'1.0','License':'UNKNOWN'},{'Name':'x','Version':'1','License':''},{'Name':'y','Version':'1'}], open('/tmp/lic.json','w'))
spec=importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('lg','scripts/license_gate.py'); m=importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(m)
print('exit', m.main('/tmp/lic.json'))  # -> exit 0, gate passes a fully-unknown-license set
PY
findings.suggested_fix: Fail (or quarantine to an explicit allowlist file) any package whose normalized license is in {'', 'UNKNOWN', 'UNKNOWN LICENSE'} or None. Add a _REVIEW set and collect such packages into a separate 'unclassified' bucket that fails the gate unless the package id is listed in a reviewed allowlist (mirroring .pip-audit-ignore). At minimum, treat unparsable licenses as deny-by-default rather than allow-by-default.

findings.title: Workflow defines no top-level permissions: — GITHUB_TOKEN runs with the repo-default (often write) scope
findings.description: None of the jobs (core, integration, audit, all-checks) declare a permissions: block, and there is no workflow-level default. The job token therefore inherits the repository/organization default, which on many repos is the legacy read-write 'permissive' default granting contents:write, packages:write, etc. The audit job runs third-party tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses, uv) and executes 'pip install -e .' over fully-resolved dependencies; a compromised build/dependency in that step would run with whatever the default token allows (e.g. pushing commits, publishing packages). Least-privilege for CI requires an explicit permissions: contents: read at the workflow level.
findings.poc: grep -n 'permissions' .github/workflows/ci.yml  # -> no match. With the org/repo default set to read-write, $GITHUB_TOKEN in the audit job (which installs and imports the entire dependency tree) carries contents:write/packages:write — an injected import-time payload could use it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a top-level 'permissions: { contents: read }' block (and grant any narrower per-job scopes only where needed, e.g. upload-artifact needs none beyond the default). This makes the token least-privilege regardless of the repo/org default.

findings.title: Integration gate reports green even when the postgres/qdrant service containers never came up — live tests silently skip
findings.description: The integration job declares postgres:16 and qdrant service containers and the workflow comment asserts the live checkpointer + vector-store tests 'run for real via the service containers'. But every live test is guarded by skipif(not postgres_up()/qdrant_up()), which probe localhost:5432 / :6333 with a 1s TCP connect and SKIP (not fail) when unreachable. If a service image fails to pull, the health check never passes, or the port mapping breaks, all 12+ live persistence/observability tests skip and pytest still exits 0 — so the integration job (a required check via all-checks) goes green while exercising none of the real backends it claims to cover. The reproducibility/coverage guarantee is unenforced.
findings.poc: In tests/integration/conftest.py postgres_up()/qdrant_up() return False on any OSError; the live tests use @pytest.mark.skipif(not postgres_up(), ...). grep shows no ASSAY_REQUIRE_*/xfail_strict/no-skip enforcement in CI or pyproject. Break the qdrant service port mapping in ci.yml -> test_qdrant_* SKIP, job still succeeds.
findings.suggested_fix: In the CI integration lane only, set an env flag (e.g. ASSAY_REQUIRE_LIVE_BACKENDS=1) and have conftest convert the skip into a hard failure when it is set (or assert postgres_up()/qdrant_up() in a session-scoped autouse fixture gated on that flag). This keeps local/dev runs skipping cleanly while guaranteeing CI actually ran the live paths.

findings.title: Provenance-trail artifact tempfile is leaked on every tracked run (delete=False, never unlinked)
findings.description: On successful completion run_study persists the provenance trail to a NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) and logs it as an MLflow artifact, but the file is never removed afterward. MLflow copies the file into the artifact store, so the original temp file remains in the system temp dir indefinitely. Every successful run that has a tracker leaks one *_provenance.json temp file; over a long-lived node (this is the production compute node) these accumulate without bound. The sibling persist_trail() path is caller-controlled, but this internal artifact path has no cleanup.
findings.poc: Run run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) N times; ls $TMPDIR/*_provenance.json shows N leaked files. The tempfile is created at pipeline.py:739 with delete=False and there is no os.unlink in the _log_trail closure or the surrounding finally.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the log_artifact call in try/finally and os.unlink(path) after logging (MLflow has already copied it), or write into a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() context that auto-cleans. Since this runs inside track() which already swallows exceptions, unlinking in a finally is safe.

findings.title: Persisted provenance records carry no key/algorithm marker — a keyed (HMAC) trail is indistinguishable from an unkeyed one when re-verified
findings.description: entries_to_records()/to_records() serialize seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash but record nothing about whether the chain was sealed with SHA-256 (unkeyed) or HMAC-SHA256 (keyed). from_records()/verify_records() require the caller to supply the correct secret out-of-band; if a verifier omits it (or passes None) on a trail that was actually keyed, verification fails with the generic 'content was altered, or the wrong key was used' error, conflating a missing-key configuration with genuine tampering. Conversely there is no recorded signal that a persisted trail SHOULD have been keyed, so a downstream consumer cannot detect that an audit-grade (cross-trust-boundary) trail was persisted unkeyed. This weakens the provenance-completeness claim for persisted trails.
findings.poc: trail = ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); trail.record('a','b'); recs = trail.to_records(); from_records(recs)  # raises ProvenanceError as if tampered, because no secret was passed and nothing in recs indicates one is required.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a non-authenticated-but-informative envelope alongside the records (e.g. {'algo':'hmac-sha256'|'sha256','keyed':bool}) so a verifier can fail with a precise 'this trail is keyed; supply the secret' message and a consumer can detect an unkeyed audit trail. Do not include the secret; the marker is metadata, not the MAC. Optionally fold an algorithm tag into the hashed preimage so it is itself integrity-protected.

findings.title: No concurrency group — superseded PR pushes keep running, and audit/lockfile drift can race
findings.description: The workflow has no concurrency: block, so every push to an open PR spawns a full matrix (core x4 python + integration + audit) without cancelling the prior in-flight run. Beyond wasted minutes, two overlapping audit runs on the same branch both run 'uv pip compile' and 'pip install -e .', and concurrent SBOM/license artifact uploads with identical names can race. For a single dedicated compute node this also compounds resource contention with the reasoning pool / checkpoint pool described in the codebase.
findings.poc: grep -n concurrency .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. Push twice in quick succession to a PR; both runs execute to completion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a top-level concurrency: { group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: true } so a newer commit cancels the stale run and only one audit/lockfile-compile executes per ref at a time.

findings.title: pip-audit and SBOM each re-fetch the advisory DB / re-resolve — a network blip fails the audit gate non-reproducibly despite the reproducibility intent
findings.description: The audit job runs pip-audit three separate times (gate, SBOM generation, and implicitly via the second resolution) plus a license-gate 'pip install -e .', each reaching out to PyPI / the OSV-Vuln DB at run time. The stated goal is a reproducible supply-chain gate, but the result still depends on live network availability of the advisory service and PyPI at job time: a transient OSV/PyPI outage fails a commit that passed yesterday and will pass tomorrow — the same non-reproducibility class the uv/pip-audit version pins were meant to remove. There is no retry, no vulnerability-DB caching/pinning, and the SBOM step re-runs the whole audit instead of reusing the gate's result.
findings.poc: The gate step (line 131) and SBOM step (line 151) both invoke pip-audit -r requirements.lock, each performing its own vulnerability-service lookup; line 168 additionally does a fresh hashed install. No --cache-dir reuse or retry wrapper. An OSV/PyPI 5xx during any of these fails the required audit job.
findings.suggested_fix: Run pip-audit once with --cache-dir (or --vulnerability-service osv with a pinned/cached DB) and emit both the pass/fail result and the CycloneDX SBOM from that single invocation (pip-audit can output a format while auditing); wrap network steps in a bounded retry. This both halves PyPI/OSV calls and removes the duplicate live resolution from the 'reproducible' gate.

findings.title: stable_seed claims "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" but truncates SHA-256 to 32 bits
findings.description: The docstring of stable_seed states unconditionally that "distinct inputs always yield the same seed ... and distinct inputs get distinct seeds." The implementation returns int(sha256_hexdigest,16) % (1 << bits) with bits defaulting to 32. Modulo into a 32-bit space cannot give distinct seeds for distinct inputs: by the birthday bound a collision becomes more-likely-than-not at ~77,000 distinct inputs, and is guaranteed past 2^32 inputs. Since build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from this for every baseline, two different corpora/configs can collide onto the same seed, contradicting the stated determinism property (and the METHODOLOGY.md "fixed seeds ... so the same inputs reproduce the same baseline" framing relies on the inverse not being implied). The collision-resistance over-claim should be removed or scoped.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import stable_seed
# Two distinct inputs colliding in a small bit-space is trivially demonstrable by reducing bits;
# at the default 32 bits the collision probability is non-zero and grows with corpus count:
assert stable_seed('a', bits=8) != stable_seed('b', bits=8)  # may fail — only 256 buckets
# At bits=32, generate ~10^5 distinct part-strings and a duplicate seed will appear (birthday bound).
findings.suggested_fix: Soften the docstring to state the truthful property — "the same inputs always yield the same seed; collisions between distinct inputs are possible because the digest is reduced to `bits` bits" — or widen the default seed space / document the collision probability so callers do not rely on uniqueness.

findings.title: make_gate_node comment claims trail + graph state are recorded "atomically" — they cannot be
findings.description: The recorder block comment asserts "the validated decision must land in the hash-chained trail and the graph state atomically." The code records to the provenance trail first, then `return update` so LangGraph commits the gate_decisions state afterward as a separate write to a separate store (checkpoint DB). There is no shared transaction across the two stores. The code only handles one failure direction (recorder raises -> do not return the update). The opposite divergence is unprotected: if the recorder succeeds and the subsequent LangGraph checkpoint write (or the process) fails, the tamper-evident trail contains the approval while graph state does not. "Atomically" overstates a best-effort, single-direction ordering guarantee.
findings.poc: Wire make_gate_node with a recorder, resume a parked gate with a valid approval, and induce a checkpointer write failure (or kill the process) immediately after the node returns but before the checkpoint commits. The trail holds the 'gate approved' entry; gate_decisions in the recovered checkpoint does not — the two stores diverge, contradicting the 'atomically' claim.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace "atomically" with the accurate guarantee, e.g. "records to the trail BEFORE returning the state update so a recorder failure aborts the decision; the trail write and the LangGraph state commit are separate stores and are NOT transactionally atomic — a crash between them can leave the trail ahead of graph state."

findings.title: "Terminal-decision idempotency" is a no-op only on graph state, not on the recorded trail
findings.description: The module docstring claims "Terminal-decision idempotency. An `approved` decision is terminal: re-entering an already-approved gate is a no-op (it does not re-prompt or re-record)." already_decided() determines this purely from state['gate_decisions']. The recorder path (the hash-chained trail) is never consulted. Because the trail and graph state are not atomic (see finding B) and the trail has no de-duplication, a re-entry where graph state lost the prior approval but the trail retained it will pass already_decided()==False and call recorder() again — appending a duplicate 'gate approved' entry to the append-only trail. So the documented "does not re-record" idempotency does not hold for the trail in exactly the recovery scenario the gate-node design exists to handle.
findings.poc: 1) Resume a gate with an approval (recorder writes trail entry, returns update). 2) Restore/replay graph state from a checkpoint taken before the gate_decisions update landed (so state has no approval) while keeping the same trail. 3) Re-enter the node: already_decided() returns False, the operator is re-prompted, and on approval recorder() appends a second 'approved' entry — re-recording, contradicting the docstring.
findings.suggested_fix: Scope the claim to graph state: "re-entering an already-approved gate is a no-op with respect to LangGraph graph state" and note that trail de-duplication is not provided, or key already_decided() off the durable trail as well so the no-op covers re-recording.

findings.title: redact_creds docstring claims the primary connection string is "always scrubbed precisely"
findings.description: redact_creds documents "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely" via layer 1 (text.replace(conn_str, sanitized)). Layer 1 is an exact substring match against the literal conn_str. psycopg/libpq error messages do not always echo the DSN verbatim: a password can be URL-decoded, re-quoted, partially rendered, or the host/port reformatted, so the exact-match replace can miss the credential and only the generic backstops (layers 2-3) catch it — and those are explicitly described as best-effort. The word "always" promises a precision the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering.
findings.poc: Construct a conn_str with a percent-encoded password (e.g. password 'a%2Fb') so libpq decodes it before embedding the decoded form in an error string. text.replace(conn_str, ...) does not match the decoded substring; layer 1 misses it and only the regex backstops (best-effort) apply — so the primary credential is not 'always scrubbed precisely'.
findings.suggested_fix: Change "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely" to "...is scrubbed precisely when the backend echoes it verbatim; the URI-userinfo and DSN-password regex layers backstop other renderings (best-effort)."

findings.title: numpy resample path claimed "exactly equal" / "never a semantic change" — diverges on NaN in null
findings.description: _resample_stability documents that the numpy path "computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts ... so its result is exactly equal to the Python path; it is purely a speed optimization ... never a semantic change." This holds for finite values but not for NaN. The pure-Python path uses `v >= observed` / `v <= observed`, where any NaN comparison is False (NaN never counts as extreme). The numpy path uses np.sort + np.searchsorted on a float64 array; numpy sorts NaN to the end and searchsorted against that ordering yields different extreme counts than the Python comparison count. With a NaN present in `null` the two paths can produce different p-values and therefore different stability fractions — a semantic divergence the docstring rules out.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import _resample_stability
null = [0.0, 1.0, float('nan'), 2.0]
resamples = [1.5]
# Python path: counts only finite v >= 1.5 -> extreme=1 ; numpy path: NaN sorted last shifts searchsorted index -> different extreme count, so the two paths can disagree on (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha.
findings.suggested_fix: Either pre-validate that `null`/`resamples` contain no NaN (raise loudly, consistent with the engine's reproducibility stance), or qualify the docstring: "identical for finite inputs; NaN values are not supported and the two paths may differ."

findings.title: LocalDataVersioner.put documents atomic/idempotent publish but the guarantee is filesystem-scoped and undocumented as such in the class header
findings.description: put() documents "Publish is atomic ... os.replace-d into place, so a reader never observes a partial artifact" and the class header says "Deterministic and fully offline." os.replace is atomic only within a single filesystem; the temp file is created in dest.parent (same dir) so this holds in practice, but the dest.exists() fast-path (line 71) plus copy means the idempotency/atomicity claim silently degrades if ASSAY_DATA_STORE spans a mount boundary via symlink or the parent dir is on a different device than the temp (not possible here, but the broad "atomic" wording invites misuse by adapters that override path layout). More concretely, the claim "a reader never observes a partial artifact" ignores that a concurrent reader can observe dest.exists()==False then read after another writer's replace — the content-addressed design makes this harmless, but the absolute "never" wording is stronger than what the code structurally proves for arbitrary callers.
findings.poc: Point ASSAY_DATA_STORE at a path where dest.parent and the OS temp differ in device (or have an adapter subclass place the temp elsewhere); os.replace then raises OSError(EXDEV) instead of atomically publishing — contradicting the unconditional "Publish is atomic" statement. The shipped code keeps the temp in dest.parent, so the claim is true for the default but the docstring presents it as an invariant of the method rather than of the same-filesystem temp placement.
findings.suggested_fix: Qualify: "Publish is atomic on a single filesystem (the temp file is created in the destination's own directory so os.replace stays intra-filesystem)." and soften "a reader never observes a partial artifact" to note it relies on intra-filesystem os.replace.

findings.title: Malformed reasoning params leak a raw ValueError/TypeError through the typed public boundary (run/run_json)
findings.description: ReasoningSeam.run / run_json are documented as the single hardened abstraction over LLM execution, returning typed errors (ReasoningError / PermanentReasoningError / RateLimitError) so callers can classify transient vs permanent failures and so _run_with_retries can decide whether to retry. However _attempt() coerces caller-supplied request.params inline BEFORE entering any tier backend: `temperature = float(p.get('temperature', 0.0))` (line 512), `seed = p.get('_seed')` then `int(seed)` (lines 515/527), and `json_mode = bool(...)`. If a caller passes a non-numeric temperature ('hot'), a None temperature, or a non-integer _seed, `float()`/`int()` raises a bare ValueError/TypeError. That exception is raised inside _attempt, which runs under _run_with_retries — whose except clauses catch ONLY PermanentReasoningError/RateLimitError/ReasoningError. A raw ValueError/TypeError therefore propagates straight out of run()/run_json() unwrapped, violating the module's typed-error contract (lines 1-26, 641-647). A caller doing `try: seam.run(req) except ReasoningError:` will not catch it and will crash. The error is also semantically permanent (a malformed param is not retryable), so it should be a PermanentReasoningError, not allowed to escape untyped.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.reasoning.seam import TieredReasoningSeam, ReasoningRequest, StakesTier, ReasoningError
seam = TieredReasoningSeam()
for params in ({'temperature':'hot'}, {'temperature':None}, {'_seed':'notint'}):
    req = ReasoningRequest(prompt='x', tier=StakesTier.BULK, purpose='t', params=params)
    try:
        seam.run(req)
    except ReasoningError:
        print(params, '-> typed (good)')
    except Exception as e:
        print(params, '-> RAW LEAK', type(e).__name__)
# Output:
# {'temperature': 'hot'} -> RAW LEAK ValueError
# {'temperature': None}   -> RAW LEAK TypeError
# {'_seed': 'notint'}     -> RAW LEAK ValueError
findings.suggested_fix: Parse and validate the params inside _attempt under a guard that re-raises as PermanentReasoningError (malformed params are not transient), e.g. wrap the temperature/seed coercion: `try: temperature = float(p.get('temperature', 0.0)); seed = int(s) if (s:=p.get('_seed')) is not None else None except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc: raise PermanentReasoningError(f'invalid reasoning param: {exc}') from exc`. This keeps run()/run_json() to their documented typed-error contract and prevents a non-retryable bad-param from being mis-handled.

findings.title: QdrantVectorStore.query has no input validation and assumes every returned point carries a 'ref' payload (raw KeyError)
findings.description: QdrantVectorStore.query (line 110) is the public VectorStore.query implementation. It (1) does not validate `k` — a k<=0 is forwarded straight to the backend and surfaces whatever raw error Qdrant produces (or an empty/odd result), with no typed contract, unlike upsert() which validates len-equality and batch_size>=1 (lines 96-99); and (2) reads `p.payload['ref']` for every returned point. If the collection contains any point that was NOT written by this store's upsert() (e.g. inserted by another process/tool, or a future schema change), that point has no 'ref' key and query() raises a bare KeyError rather than a clear, typed store error. The class docstring promises ids round-trip via the 'ref' payload but never defends the read path against foreign points. Both are partial-failure / edge-case gaps at a public boundary that has otherwise been hardened (close()/ownership #117, batching #118).
findings.poc:
# Conceptual (requires a running Qdrant): a point upserted by an external writer lacks the 'ref' payload.
# QdrantVectorStore.query then does: [(p.payload['ref'], float(p.score)) for p in res.points]
# -> KeyError: 'ref' escapes query() untyped.
# Separately: store.query(vector, k=0) forwards k=0 to query_points with no guard, unlike upsert's batch_size<1 check.
findings.suggested_fix: Validate k at the top of query (`if k < 1: raise ValueError('k must be >= 1')`) to mirror upsert's guard, and read the payload defensively: `ref = (p.payload or {}).get('ref'); if ref is None: continue  # or raise a typed store error` so a foreign point cannot crash the query with a raw KeyError.

findings.title: verdict_from_pvalue docstring claims 'inputs are validated' but never checks statistic finiteness
findings.description: verdict_from_pvalue (line 101) is a public function whose docstring states 'Inputs are validated (issue #4)'. It validates p_value (_validate_pvalue) and alpha (_validate_alpha) but does NOT validate `statistic` for finiteness. The two confirm_* entrypoints DO call `_validate_finite('statistic'/'observed', ...)` before delegating, so the contract holds when reached through them — but a caller invoking verdict_from_pvalue directly (it is exported and documented) can pass a NaN/Inf statistic and it will be recorded into the verdict's evidence/provenance unchecked. Because statistic is documented as 'recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the verdict)', the impact is a non-finite value polluting the provenance/determinism record rather than a wrong verdict — hence low — but it is a documented-validation contract that does not fully hold at this boundary.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import verdict_from_pvalue
v = verdict_from_pvalue('h1', statistic=float('nan'), p_value=0.01, alpha=0.05, powered=True, direction_supports_claim=True)
print(v.statistic)  # nan recorded; no ValueError raised despite docstring 'inputs are validated'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_validate_finite('statistic', statistic)` at the top of verdict_from_pvalue alongside the existing _validate_pvalue/_validate_alpha calls, so the documented validation contract holds for direct callers (and a non-finite statistic cannot enter the provenance/determinism record).

findings.title: Module-import-time int() of ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT (and tracing port) raises an opaque ValueError masquerading as an import failure
findings.description: VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get('ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT', '6333')) executes at module import (line 18); observability/tracing.py does the same for ASSAY_TRACING_PORT (line 30). A non-numeric value for either env var raises ValueError during `import`, which surfaces to the user as an import-time crash with no context tying it to the offending env var — and, for any module that imports these transitively, can present as a confusing ImportError-adjacent failure rather than a clear configuration error. The codebase elsewhere takes care to re-read and bound numeric env config at point-of-use with clamping (e.g. seam.py _env/max(), checkpoint.py pool bounds), so this import-time unbounded parse is an inconsistency in the config-error contract. Edge severity is low because it only triggers on misconfiguration, but the failure mode is opaque.
findings.poc:
ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=notaport python -c 'import assay_engine.persistence.vectorstore'
# -> ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notaport'  (at import, no env-var context)
findings.suggested_fix: Parse these ports lazily inside vector_store_url()/get_qdrant_client() and tracing_endpoint(), or wrap the int() in a helper that raises a clear configuration error naming the env var, e.g. `def _port(name, default): raw = os.environ.get(name, default); ...; raise ValueError(f'{name} must be an integer port; got {raw!r}')`. This keeps import side-effect-free and gives an actionable error.

result.reasoning:
The finding's central factual claim is false on the actual repo. Running the PoC command exactly on the repo's interpreter (Python 3.14.5, /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv) yields:

  $ PYTHONHASHSEED=2 .venv/bin/python -c "print(list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})))"
  ['b', 1, 2, 'a']

This is EXACTLY what the comment at tests/test_immutability.py:65-66 documents ("raw frozenset iteration ... is ['b',1,2,'a'] (verified)") — NOT [1, 2, 'b', 'a'] as the PoC asserts. The PoC's headline ("documented 'verified' fact is stale/wrong ... [1, 2, 'b', 'a'], not ['b',1,2,'a']") is simply incorrect against the code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay; it appears to have been produced against a different interpreter/build.

The discriminating property the finding worries about also holds robustly: at seed=2 the raw order ['b',1,2,'a'] != stable [1,2,'a','b'] (verified True), so the guard is NOT vacuous. I simulated the buggy revert (raw iteration) under the pinned seed and it produces ['b',1,2,'a'], which differs from stable — the guard would fail every run, exactly as intended. The test passes (1 passed in 0.06s).

The only kernel of truth is the generic, speculative observation that a single hardcoded PYTHONHASHSEED is a version dependency that a hypothetical future CPython could neutralize. But (a) that is forward-looking speculation, not a demonstrated defect, (b) the comment is verified-correct on the repo's pinned interpreter, and (c) the test is currently fully discriminating. The concrete, checkable assertions in the finding (the wrong raw order, the "stale/wrong comment") are false. Finding does not survive.

Verified against source; could not disprove. (1) Signature asymmetry is real: adjudicate_with_baseline (src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:193, 264-267) accepts on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None and calls it in-loop after each verdict; discover_and_confirm (src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py:69-76) has signature (corpus, split, *, discover, confirm, authority) -> list[Verdict] with no hook. (2) Exhaustive grep of src/ and tests/ shows on_step exists ONLY in adjudication — discovery offers no callback, generator, or yield; its loop (discovery.py:133-164) appends to a local list and returns it whole. (3) The pipeline confirms the consequence rather than refuting it: it does NOT call discover_and_confirm at all. It hand-reimplements the discovery/preregister/confirm loop inline (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:496-584) precisely so it can trail.record() per hypothesis (538-544) and per verdict (577-583) in order — i.e. the exact 're-implement the loop' workaround the finding cites. Meanwhile the adjudication phase reuses the shared runner via on_step (pipeline.py:660-668). The engine itself exhibits the asymmetry. (4) A standalone caller of discover_and_confirm receives only the final list[Verdict] and cannot record (hypothesis, verdict) provenance in confirmation order before the next step runs (GOVERNANCE §3 append-before-next-step ordering), and the returned verdicts lack the preregistration digest/locked_at the trail needs. No contract or test makes the PoC moot.

I tried to disprove this and could not. Both factual claims in the finding are verified against the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_immutability.py and src/assay_engine/_frozen.py.

CLAIM 1 (probabilistic in-process guard): test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (lines 43-48) asserts unfreeze(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) == [1,2,'a','b'] at the ambient (unpinned) PYTHONHASHSEED. The implementation (_frozen.py:158-161) sorts by (type name, repr) so it passes today. But as a regression guard against a revert to raw `list(value)` iteration, it is probabilistic: enumerating seeds, raw frozenset iteration equals the stable order on a sizeable fraction (e.g. seeds 4, 8, 10 in just 0-10). The test's own comment at lines 52-53 concedes the in-process assertion "can pass ~1/3 of the time even on the buggy raw-iteration code." Confirmed.

CLAIM 2 (redundant assertion): lines 47 and 48 use {1,'a',2,'b'} and {'b',2,'a',1}. I verified these are the SAME set object with identical per-process iteration order (both iterate ['a','b',2,1] in this process), so line 48 adds zero independent coverage and gives a false impression of testing two orderings. Confirmed.

Strongest refutation attempt: the sibling test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess (lines 51-69) pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess, where raw iteration yields [1,2,'b','a'] (verified) which differs from the stable order, so a raw-iteration revert fails that test EVERY run. That sibling does make the overall G-004 protection deterministic. HOWEVER, the finding does not claim the overall guard is broken — it explicitly cites the subprocess test's comment. The finding is a narrowly-scoped test-quality defect report about the specific in-process test, and git history (f5a350f "remediate the 1 confirmation CONCERN — G-004 test discrimination") shows the remediation ADDED the subprocess test but left both flagged defects in place: lines 47/48 are still the identical set object (pure redundancy, independent of any subprocess test), and lines 43-48 remain a probabilistic standalone guard. Both defects are objectively present in current code; I cannot disprove them. The finding survives, though it is a low-severity test-hygiene issue, not a behavioral bug.

Confirmed against the actual code; could not disprove.

I attempted to refute this from the code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py and could not. The accounting is exactly as described: _inflight is incremented in _submit_bounded (line 232) before _pool.submit, and the ONLY decrement paths are (a) the submit-failure rollbacks (lines 237, 241 — reached only if submit/callback-attach itself raises) and (b) _release (line 216). _release is invoked solely via future.add_done_callback(_release) (line 251) or the interrupt-window fallback (line 253). Therefore the slot is freed only when the future COMPLETES. The timeout path in _with_timeout (lines 263-267) deliberately does NOT release the slot ("The worker keeps its slot until the call returns") — correct for a hung-but-alive worker, since the future will eventually complete and fire the callback. But there is no path that decrements _inflight when a future NEVER completes (worker thread vanishes via a C-level crash that doesn't kill the process, or executor state corruption). I searched for any self-heal — watchdog, periodic sweeper, _inflight reset on saturation, or pool re-creation — and found none. Once _inflight reaches _POOL_WORKERS (8) from such leaks, _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError('saturated') unconditionally (lines 224-231) for the rest of the process lifetime, bricking run()/run_json() for BOTH tiers (is_available's BULK urllib probe survives, but the actual execution path does not). The PoC (monkeypatch _pool.submit to return an unresolved Future, call 8x to leak all slots, 9th raises) is a faithful simulation of this condition. Tests (tests/test_reasoning.py:215-277) cover saturation-is-retryable, shutdown-normalization, and slot-release under real threads/timeouts — but NONE cover a vanished/never-completing future, so no contract or test makes the PoC moot. The only counterargument is probability (a true worker C-crash usually kills the whole process, prompting a restart; pure-Python work items always complete the future via _WorkItem.run's try/except), but that is a likelihood argument, not a correctness refutation. The finding itself scopes the trigger to sub-Python-level worker death and labels it a latent risk, which is accurate and not overstated. I cannot disprove it.

Confirmed against the actual code; could not disprove. At /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:733-746, the _log_trail closure creates tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("w", suffix="_provenance.json", delete=False, ...). The with-block only closes the handle; with delete=False, closing does NOT unlink the file. It then calls t.log_artifact(rid, path). MlflowExperimentTracker.log_artifact (src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py:92-93) merely delegates to MLflow's client.log_artifact, which copies the file into the artifact store and leaves the source temp file in place. The track() helper (pipeline.py:317-324) only invokes fn and traps exceptions — no cleanup. The surrounding finally block (pipeline.py:771-778) only closes the trace ctx and calls tracker.end_run; it never unlinks the temp file. A repo-wide grep for unlink/os.remove shows the only such call is in persistence/versioning.py:81 (an unrelated atomic-replace path), not in the provenance-artifact path. No test asserts cleanup (test_pipeline.py:451-454 only asserts the artifact was logged). Therefore every successful run with a tracker leaks one *_provenance.json file in the system temp dir, unbounded over a long-lived node. The PoC is accurate. Minor caveat: actual unboundedness on a real node depends on OS tmp-reaper policy (e.g., systemd-tmpfiles / macOS periodic cleanup may eventually remove old files), but the code itself performs no cleanup, so the defect as described is real.

Confirmed accurate against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py. The recorder writes to the hash-chained provenance trail (line 152) BEFORE `return update` (line 159), after which LangGraph commits `gate_decisions` to a separate checkpoint store. These are two independent stores: there is no shared transaction, two-phase commit, or reconciliation logic anywhere (grep for atomic/two-phase/reconcile/rollback/compensate across src/ found only unrelated file-write and registry-lock atomicity, nothing tying the trail to the checkpoint). The code only guards failure-direction A: if recorder raises, it raises GateError and does not return the update (lines 153-158), so graph state cannot get the decision when the trail lacks it. The opposite direction is unprotected — if recorder succeeds and the subsequent checkpoint write or the process fails after the node returns, the trail holds 'approved' while gate_decisions does not. The trail is append-only and hash-chained (provenance.py: self._entries.append at line 159), so it cannot be rolled back to compensate; this divergence is by design irreversible. The comment's claim that the decision lands in trail and graph state 'atomically' (gatenode.py:145) overstates a best-effort, single-direction ordering guarantee, exactly as the finding states. The PoC (induce checkpointer write failure or kill the process after node return but before checkpoint commit) reproduces the divergence. I could not disprove it.

Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py and provenance.py. All three load-bearing claims hold:

1. already_decided() (gatenode.py:79-84) determines terminality purely from state['gate_decisions']; it never consults the recorder or the hash-chained trail.

2. The node (gatenode.py:122-159) only short-circuits via already_decided(). If that returns False it re-fires interrupt() and, on approval, calls recorder() for each record (lines 143-159). The record-before-return atomicity comment (lines 144-158) only guarantees trail+state consistency WITHIN one node execution; it provides nothing across a checkpoint restore that drops the gate_decisions update while keeping the trail.

3. The trail has no de-duplication: ProvenanceTrail.record() (provenance.py:116-160) is documented as "The only way to add to the trail" and unconditionally appends (self._entries.append(entry), line 159). verify()/verify_records() only check hash-chain integrity, not semantic uniqueness, so a duplicate 'gate approved' entry forms a perfectly valid chain.

Therefore the PoC scenario is realizable: restore graph state from a checkpoint taken before the gate_decisions update landed while retaining the same trail → already_decided()==False → operator re-prompted → recorder() appends a SECOND 'approved' entry. This directly contradicts the module docstring's claim (line 21-22) that an approved decision "does not re-prompt or re-record," and it occurs in exactly the recovery scenario the gate node exists to handle. I could find no code path (no trail consultation in already_decided, no recorder-side dedup, no state/trail atomic binding across restore) that makes the PoC impossible or moot. Finding survives.

Confirmed against the actual code. determinism.py:69-76: stable_seed returns int(sha256_hexdigest,16) % (1 << bits) with bits=32 default, while its docstring (line 73) unconditionally claims "distinct inputs get distinct seeds." Reducing a hash modulo a 32-bit space cannot guarantee injectivity, so the universal claim is false. I empirically reproduced collisions for the exact pure function logic: distinct inputs '15'/'24' collide at bits=8 (both -> 219), and '390'/'2799' collide at bits=24 (both -> 15272228), validating the birthday-bound argument; at 32 bits a collision is more-likely-than-not past ~77k inputs and guaranteed past 2^32. The only test (tests/test_baseline.py:58-61, test_stable_seed_is_deterministic_and_distinct) checks a single hand-picked pair ("x","y" vs "x","z") plus range bounds — it does NOT establish the universal distinctness property, so it does not scope or refute the over-claim. build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:153) derives the reproducibility seed from these hashes via stable_seed, so two distinct corpora/configs can collide onto the same seed, contradicting the stated determinism contract. No code path, contract, or test makes the PoC impossible or moot. The fix (removing/scoping the collision-resistance claim, or widening bits) is warranted. Note: this is a docstring over-claim, not a runtime correctness bug — collisions don't corrupt reproducibility (same input still yields same seed), they only break the inverse uniqueness implication — but the finding as stated (the over-claim) survives.

Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/provenance.py and confirmed by executing the PoC with the project's .venv python. All three claims hold and I could not disprove any.

1. No key/algorithm marker: entries_to_records() (provenance.py:219-233) serializes exactly seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash. PoC output confirms record keys are precisely those seven — nothing distinguishes an HMAC-SHA256 (keyed) chain from a SHA-256 (unkeyed) one.

2. Generic error conflates missing-key with tampering: from_records(recs) on a trail built with secret=b'x'*16 raised "ProvenanceError: provenance entry 0 content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)" (provenance.py:252-256), verbatim as the finding states.

3. No signal a persisted trail SHOULD be keyed: the persistence path in pipeline.py _log_trail (lines 733-744) dumps only entries_to_records(...) as JSON to the experiment tracker — no sidecar, no flag. A downstream auditor cannot detect that an audit-grade trail was persisted unkeyed.

Refutation attempts (all failed): (a) searched src/ and tests/ for any marker/algo/keyed field or sidecar carrying the secret or algorithm — none exists; the secret is always passed out-of-band to verify_records/from_records. (b) The behavior is not an accidental gap that some other code path covers — it is the explicitly tested contract: test_provenance.py:86-87 (from_records(recs) without secret on a keyed trail raises) and test_pipeline.py:680 assert exactly this failure mode, so there is no hidden mitigation. (c) The module docstring (lines 16-28) and verify_records docstring (line 244) document that the caller must supply the secret out-of-band, but documenting the limitation does not make the absent marker or the error conflation untrue. The weakness to the provenance-completeness claim for persisted trails is real.

Finding survives refutation. The docstring at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:140 states "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely," attributing this to layer 1. Layer 1 is literally `text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))` (line 143) — an exact substring match against the verbatim conn_str. The PoC is technically sound: psycopg/libpq error messages are PQerrorMessage strings not guaranteed to echo the DSN verbatim. With a percent-encoded password (e.g. `postgresql://admin:a%2Fb@host/db`), libpq URL-decodes `%2Fb`→`/` before use; if the error text renders the decoded form (`a/b`) or reformats into keyword/value form, the literal substring `a%2Fb` is absent and `replace` is a no-op. Layer 1 then misses entirely, leaving only layers 2-3, which the docstring itself labels "best-effort." So "always ... precisely" overstates layer 1's guarantee. Attempts to disprove failed: (1) No upstream contract forces libpq to echo the verbatim DSN. (2) The only relevant test, test_redact_creds_strips_passwords_with_special_chars in tests/test_persistence.py:35-41, embeds the conn_str verbatim into the message (`msg = f\"...{conn}...\"`), which trivially satisfies the literal match and does NOT exercise a decoded/reformatted rendering — so no test enforces the 'always precise' claim. (3) _sanitize_conn_str only affects the replacement string, not the match. The word 'always' promises precision the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering; this is an accurate documentation-accuracy finding.

Refuted against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py. (1) The finding's headline claim — that the filesystem-scoping is undocumented in the class header — is moot: the class header (line 44) makes NO atomicity claim ("Deterministic and fully offline"), so there is nothing to qualify there; the atomicity claim lives only in put()'s docstring. (2) That docstring claim IS explicitly scoped: line 79 reads `os.replace(tmp, dest)  # atomic publish within the same filesystem`. (3) The same-filesystem precondition is structurally enforced: line 74 creates the temp via `tempfile.mkstemp(dir=str(dest.parent), ...)`, co-locating temp and dest, so EXDEV is impossible for shipped code. (4) The PoC self-admits it cannot run against shipped code — it requires "an adapter subclass place the temp elsewhere" and states "(not possible here)" and "the claim is true for the default." (5) DataVersioner is a structural Protocol (line 25); adapters implement the Protocol independently (module docstring line 7), not by subclassing LocalDataVersioner and overriding temp placement, so the hypothesized misuse vector is outside the contract. (6) "a reader never observes a partial artifact" holds — partial content exists only in the .tmp- file; dest is materialized only via atomic os.replace; the conceded "harmless" concurrent-reader scenario yields a complete same-digest artifact, never a partial read.
