The NaN divergence mechanism is real — I reproduced it empirically (in a venv with numpy): for null=[0.0,1.0,nan,2.0], resamples=[1.5], tail='greater', alpha=0.5 the pure-Python path returns stability 1.0 while the numpy searchsorted path returns 0.0, because np.sort puts NaN last and searchsorted then counts it toward the 'greater' extreme. So the docstring's literal "exactly equal / never a semantic change" is technically imprecise IF a NaN reaches _resample_stability.

But the finding's stated impact — "different p-values and therefore different stability fractions" in actual use — is unreachable, which moots it:

1. _resample_stability is a private helper (leading underscore). Its ONLY production caller is confirm_whole_corpus (confirm.py:347,351). The PoC invokes the private function directly with raw lists, bypassing the engine contract.

2. confirm_whole_corpus finite-validates every input before the call: null_distribution at lines 329-330 (`for v in null_distribution: _validate_finite(...)`) and resample_statistics at lines 345-346. _validate_finite (line 96-98) uses math.isfinite, which is False for NaN, so it raises ValueError on any NaN. A NaN in `null` therefore raises at line 330 long before _resample_stability runs at line 347 — the divergent path is never entered.

3. grep confirms no other production caller of _resample_stability; the only other reference is a test.

The divergence cannot produce divergent p-values/stability fractions through any public entry point because both numpy and pure paths are only reached after NaN has been rejected. The finding's asserted behavioral consequence is impossible in the real system; what remains is at most an over-broad docstring phrasing for a direct private-call scenario the engine's contract excludes. Disproved.

I could not disprove the finding; all its factual claims verify against the actual code.

Verified facts (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py):
- BULK: `_bulk_complete` always sets `num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT` (line 368; default 2048, line 99), and the docstring lines 95-99 explicitly argue this output bound is "the real guard" because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a backend call already blocked in generation.
- HIGH_STAKES: `_high_stakes_complete` builds `ClaudeAgentOptions(system_prompt, model, permission_mode, setting_sources, allowed_tools, env)` (lines 435-442) with NO output/cost/turn bound of any kind. Its only guards are wall-clock: inner `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (line 458, default 300s) and outer `_with_timeout(..., HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT+30)` (line 550). PoC checks confirmed: `num_predict` appears only in the BULK path; `max_tokens` appears nowhere in seam.py.

Refutation attempts that failed:
1. "The SDK offers no per-call output-token cap, so an equivalent to num_predict cannot be applied." I inspected the pinned SDK (claude-agent-sdk 0.2.102, .venv). `ClaudeAgentOptions` (types.py line 1634) indeed has no `max_tokens`/`max_output_tokens` field — but it DOES expose `max_budget_usd` (line 1714: "The query will stop if this budget is exceeded") and `max_turns` (line 1708). These are available, unused guards that would bound a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation independent of wall-clock. So the asymmetry the finding describes is real and an applicable guard existed; the SDK detail only narrows the exact mechanism name, it does not moot the asymmetry.
2. "Wall-clock fully bounds it." This is exactly the argument the BULK docstring rejects (a call blocked in generation is terminated only when the full 300s elapses, consuming the entire subscription turn plus a bounded-pool worker slot for 300s, where BULK would stop at 2048 tokens well inside its window). The fail_after does cancel the subprocess, but only after the full timeout — a duration bound, not a cost/length bound.
3. No test or contract enforces a HIGH_STAKES output/cost cap; grep confirms none exists.

Caveat (not disqualifying): HIGH_STAKES is not strictly unbounded — the subprocess IS cancelled at HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT, so this is a weaker/asymmetric guard, not a total absence of any guard. The finding explicitly frames it as "bounded only by wall-clock," which is accurate, so the caveat does not invalidate it. Finding survives.

deduplicated_findings.title: alpha and stability_threshold are post-hoc free parameters, not pre-registered — threshold HARKing survives the firewall
deduplicated_findings.description: The pre-registration digest (canonical_hypothesis_digest, preregistration.py:114-118) binds statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params. Verified: `alpha` and `stability_threshold` are NOT fields on Hypothesis (hypothesis.py:61-74) and are NOT in the digest payload. They are supplied as plain runtime arguments to verdict_from_pvalue/confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level at confirmation time, and verdict_from_pvalue even CONSTRUCTS the recorded decision_rule string from the runtime alpha (confirm.py:131 `rule = f"...alpha={alpha}..."`) instead of comparing it against the locked decision_rule. Nothing reads hypothesis.decision_rule/params to constrain the confirm-time threshold. An analyst who has seen the null/baseline can therefore choose alpha=0.05 vs 0.10 or stability_threshold=0.9 vs 0.6 to flip indeterminate↔supported — exactly the post-hoc-threshold HARKing the firewall claims to prevent. The most decision-bearing numbers are not committed before confirmation, defeating the core methodological guarantee.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater' (alpha not a field, so the threshold is unlocked). After seeing the null: confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=X, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=R, stability_threshold=0.9, authority=auth) -> indeterminate ('does not beat null at alpha'). Re-run the SAME locked hypothesis (require_preregistered still passes — content unchanged) with alpha=0.10, stability_threshold=0.6 -> supported. Verdict changed purely by post-hoc threshold choice. Same applies to confirm_unit_level's alpha.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Add `alpha` (and optional `stability_threshold`) as typed fields on Hypothesis, include them in canonical_hypothesis_digest, and have the confirmers READ them from the locked hypothesis instead of accepting caller arguments — or, if kept as arguments, require they equal the locked values and raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch (mirroring _resolve_direction's cross-check). Build the recorded decision_rule from the locked value, not the runtime arg.

deduplicated_findings.title: `powered` is a trusted, unbound caller flag that gates indeterminate↔decisive after the data is seen
deduplicated_findings.description: verdict_from_pvalue takes `powered: bool` from the caller and routes `not powered -> indeterminate` (confirm.py:137-138). Verified: `powered` is neither a Hypothesis field nor part of the digest, and the engine performs no power calculation. A confirmer thus decides AFTER seeing the held-out result whether the test was 'powered enough' to be decisive. Combined with H-001 this is a second post-hoc lever: a significant-but-against-the-claim result can be neutralized to indeterminate with powered=False, or a favorable result promoted with powered=True. The docstring frames powered as a method-capability fact but nothing binds it to a pre-stated power/sample-size criterion.
deduplicated_findings.poc: For one locked unit-level hypothesis with a fixed held-out statistic/p_value: confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True) -> supported/contradicted; call again with powered=False -> indeterminate. Both pass the identical pre-registration check; decisiveness chosen post-hoc.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Require the power/decisiveness criterion (e.g. minimum n, minimum detectable effect) to be pre-registered in params (bound in the digest) and have the engine derive `powered` from the held-out sample it evaluated against that locked criterion, rather than accepting an opaque boolean. At minimum record the locked criterion in evidence and assert the supplied `powered` is consistent with it.

deduplicated_findings.title: Locked `test_name` is bound in the digest but never enforced against the test actually executed
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: test_name IS in canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:114) but is read NOWHERE except when building the digest — no confirmer or runner checks that the executed test matches the pre-registered test_name. On the unit-level path this is most acute: confirm_unit_level receives only an opaque statistic/p_value/direction_supports_claim ('the engine cannot itself infer the tail', confirm.py:163-169) and verdict_from_pvalue records a hard-coded test_description='held-out unit-level test' (confirm.py:196) rather than the locked test_name. A study can pre-register test_name='mann_whitney_u', then after seeing held-out data run whichever test gives the desired p_value and feed it in; require_preregistered passes because content (including the now-meaningless test_name) is unchanged. The docstrings claim the locked content governs the verdict; for the test choice it does not.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Lock a unit-level hypothesis with test_name='mann_whitney_u'. After seeing the held-out partition, compute p_value with a t-test that clears alpha and call confirm_unit_level(h, ..., p_value=that_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth) -> supported. require_preregistered raises nothing; the executed test silently differs from the locked one.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Have the engine-side confirmer take the test identity it ran and assert it equals hypothesis.test_name (raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch), and propagate test_name into verdict_from_pvalue's test_description. Where the engine cannot see the test (study-owned confirmers), document explicitly that test-choice integrity is an un-enforced caller obligation so the docstrings stop over-claiming.

deduplicated_findings.title: Malformed reasoning params leak a raw ValueError/TypeError through the typed public boundary (run/run_json)
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: _attempt() coerces caller params inline before any tier backend — `temperature = float(p.get('temperature', 0.0))` (seam.py:512) and `int(seed)` (seam.py:527). A non-numeric/None temperature or non-integer _seed raises a bare ValueError/TypeError inside _attempt, which runs under _run_with_retries whose except clauses catch ONLY PermanentReasoningError/RateLimitError/ReasoningError (seam.py:592). A raw ValueError/TypeError therefore propagates out of run()/run_json() unwrapped, violating the module's documented typed-error contract; a caller catching ReasoningError will crash. The error is semantically permanent (a malformed param is not retryable), so it should be a PermanentReasoningError.
deduplicated_findings.poc: seam = TieredReasoningSeam(); for params in ({'temperature':'hot'}, {'temperature':None}, {'_seed':'notint'}): req = ReasoningRequest(prompt='x', tier=StakesTier.BULK, purpose='t', params=params); seam.run(req) -> raises raw ValueError/TypeError instead of ReasoningError.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Coerce/validate temperature and _seed inside _attempt under a guard that re-raises as PermanentReasoningError, e.g. wrap the float()/int() in try/except (ValueError, TypeError) -> raise PermanentReasoningError(f'invalid reasoning param: {exc}') from exc. Keeps run()/run_json() to the documented typed-error contract.

deduplicated_findings.title: HIGH_STAKES tier lacks the output-length cap that BULK enforces (asymmetric runaway-generation guard)
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: BULK always sets num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT (seam.py:99, 368) and its docstring calls this 'the real guard' because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in generation. _high_stakes_complete (seam.py:414-503) builds ClaudeAgentOptions with NO max-output-tokens equivalent — grep for max_tokens in seam.py returns nothing. A runaway BULK generation is bounded by tokens AND wall-clock; a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation is bounded only by anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT). The cheaper/safer bound was applied only to the local tier, and HIGH_STAKES is the one consuming the fixed-cost subscription budget. The asymmetry is the same the BULK docstring argues against.
deduplicated_findings.poc: A BULK call cannot emit more than ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT (2048) tokens. The parallel HIGH_STAKES call has no analogous bound: a prompt eliciting a very long completion runs until HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default), consuming the full subscription turn and a bounded-pool worker slot.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Add an ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MAX_TOKENS cap and pass it to ClaudeAgentOptions (or the SDK's max-output-tokens option), mirroring num_predict. If the Agent SDK cannot bound output, document the asymmetry explicitly in the HIGH_STAKES docstring the way BULK's is documented.

deduplicated_findings.title: discover_and_confirm lacks the on_step provenance hook that adjudicate_with_baseline has (asymmetric audit-trail wiring)
deduplicated_findings.description: The two parallel firewall runners (discovery's own docstring calls itself 'the mirror of adjudication') were hardened asymmetrically for per-step provenance. adjudicate_with_baseline takes on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None (adjudication.py:193, 264-267) so a caller can record each pre-registered hypothesis+verdict to the hash-chained trail as each happens (#93), preserving GOVERNANCE §3's 'recorded before the next step runs' ordering. discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) has no such hook: a standalone discovery study can only batch-record after the whole loop returns, losing the append-before-next-step ordering its twin enforces.
deduplicated_findings.poc: adjudicate_with_baseline(..., on_step=lambda h,v: trail.record(...)) lands each verdict in confirmation order. discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=..., authority=...) accepts no on_step, so a standalone study records verdicts only after the loop returns or by re-implementing the loop.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Add an `on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None = None` parameter to discover_and_confirm and invoke it after each verdict is validated (right after the misattribution check at discovery.py:164), mirroring adjudication.py:264-267.

deduplicated_findings.title: License gate silently passes UNKNOWN / empty / missing-license dependencies (allow-by-default)
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: main() does `lic = (pkg.get('License') or '').upper()` (line 34) and only appends to `bad` when a deny token matches (line 37); a missing/empty/UNKNOWN field yields '' and is treated as ALLOWED. pip-licenses frequently reports UNKNOWN for packages whose license lives only in a classifier/LICENSE file. A genuinely GPL/AGPL/SSPL dependency whose metadata pip-licenses cannot parse therefore bypasses the Apache-2.0 compatibility gate entirely — the exact supply-chain blind spot the gate exists to close.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Feed the gate [{'Name':'evil','Version':'1.0','License':'UNKNOWN'}, {'License':''}, {missing License}] -> main() returns 0 ('license gate OK').
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Treat unparsable licenses as deny-by-default: fail (or quarantine to a reviewed allowlist file) any package whose normalized license is in {'', 'UNKNOWN', 'UNKNOWN LICENSE', None}. Collect such packages into an 'unclassified' bucket that fails unless explicitly allowlisted (mirroring .pip-audit-ignore).

deduplicated_findings.title: Workflow defines no top-level permissions: — GITHUB_TOKEN runs with the repo-default (often write) scope
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: grep for 'permissions' in ci.yml returns no permissions block on any job or at the workflow level. The token therefore inherits the repo/org default, which on many repos is the legacy read-write default granting contents:write/packages:write. The audit job runs third-party tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses) and does 'pip install -e .' over the fully-resolved dependency tree; an injected import-time payload in that step would run with whatever the default token allows.
deduplicated_findings.poc: grep -n 'permissions' .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. With the org/repo default set to read-write, $GITHUB_TOKEN in the audit job carries contents:write/packages:write.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Add a top-level `permissions: { contents: read }` block and grant narrower per-job scopes only where needed, making the token least-privilege regardless of the repo/org default.

deduplicated_findings.title: Integration gate reports green even when service containers never came up — live backend tests silently skip
deduplicated_findings.description: The integration job declares postgres/qdrant service containers and the workflow comment claims the live checkpointer + vector-store tests run for real, but every live test is guarded by skipif(not postgres_up()/qdrant_up()), which probe localhost with a short TCP connect and SKIP (not fail) when unreachable. If an image fails to pull, the health check never passes, or a port mapping breaks, all live persistence/observability tests skip and pytest still exits 0 — so the required integration check goes green while exercising none of the real backends it claims to cover. The reproducibility/coverage guarantee is unenforced.
deduplicated_findings.poc: conftest postgres_up()/qdrant_up() return False on OSError; live tests use skipif. No ASSAY_REQUIRE_*/xfail_strict/no-skip enforcement. Break the qdrant service port mapping -> test_qdrant_* SKIP, job still succeeds.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: In the CI integration lane only, set ASSAY_REQUIRE_LIVE_BACKENDS=1 and have conftest convert the skip into a hard failure when set (or assert postgres_up()/qdrant_up() in a session-scoped autouse fixture gated on that flag), so local/dev runs skip cleanly while CI guarantees the live paths ran.

deduplicated_findings.title: stable_seed docstring claims "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" but truncates SHA-256 to 32 bits
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: the docstring states "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" (line 14) while the implementation returns int(digest,16) % (1 << bits) with bits defaulting to 32 (line 17). Modulo into a 32-bit space cannot guarantee distinct seeds for distinct inputs — by the birthday bound a collision becomes more-likely-than-not at ~77k inputs and is guaranteed past 2^32. build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from this, so two distinct corpora/configs can collide onto the same seed, contradicting the stated property. This is a documentation over-claim, not a determinism break (the same input still maps to the same seed).
deduplicated_findings.poc: stable_seed reduces a 256-bit digest to 32 bits; generate ~10^5 distinct part-strings and a duplicate seed appears (birthday bound). At small bits it is trivially demonstrable: stable_seed('a', bits=8) and stable_seed('b', bits=8) can collide in 256 buckets.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Soften the docstring to the truthful property: "the same inputs always yield the same seed; collisions between distinct inputs are possible because the digest is reduced to `bits` bits." Optionally widen the default seed space or document the collision probability.

deduplicated_findings.title: Graph gate node warns-but-allows a missing trail recorder while run_study's gate path always records (asymmetric audit guarantee)
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: in run_study the gate handler always records to the trail before enforcing (pipeline.py run_gate). The LangGraph graph path make_gate_node (gatenode.py:90-161) makes the trail recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it merely warns (lines 114-115) and records only into non-tamper-evident LangGraph state. The docstring itself (lines 108-112) calls this a 'GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. The same governance concern is structurally guaranteed on one path and merely warned-about on the other.
deduplicated_findings.poc: make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (only a warning). Operator approvals then live solely in LangGraph checkpoint state; verify_records cannot detect a checkpoint tamper that flips the decision.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Make the recorder mandatory on make_gate_node (raise when None), matching run_study's unconditional trail.record — or, if a recorder-less node must remain supported, gate it behind an explicit opt-out flag (allow_unrecorded=True) so shipping the audit-gap configuration is a conscious choice.

deduplicated_findings.title: gatenode over-claims: "atomically" recorded trail+state, and "terminal-decision idempotency" that holds only for graph state
deduplicated_findings.description: Two related docstring over-claims, verified. (1) The recorder block asserts the validated decision lands in the hash-chained trail and graph state 'atomically' (~line 144), but the code records to the trail first then returns the update for LangGraph to commit to a separate store; there is no shared transaction. Only the recorder-raises direction is handled — if the recorder succeeds and the subsequent checkpoint write (or process) fails, the trail holds the approval while graph state does not. (2) The module docstring (line 21) claims 'an approved decision is terminal: re-entering ... does not re-prompt or re-record', but already_decided() (line 79) reads only state['gate_decisions'] — never the trail. Since the two stores are not atomic and the trail has no de-dup, a re-entry where graph state lost the prior approval but the trail retained it passes already_decided()==False and the recorder appends a SECOND 'approved' entry to the append-only trail, contradicting 'does not re-record'.
deduplicated_findings.poc: (B) Wire a recorder, resume a parked gate with a valid approval, kill the process after the node returns but before the checkpoint commits: trail holds 'approved', recovered checkpoint gate_decisions does not. (C) Restore graph state from a checkpoint taken before the gate_decisions update landed while keeping the same trail; re-enter -> already_decided() False -> re-prompt -> recorder appends a duplicate 'approved' entry.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'atomically' with the accurate guarantee ('records to the trail BEFORE returning the update so a recorder failure aborts the decision; the two stores are NOT transactional — a crash between them can leave the trail ahead of graph state'). Scope the idempotency claim to graph state, or key already_decided() off the durable trail as well so the no-op also covers re-recording.

deduplicated_findings.title: Provenance-trail artifact tempfile leaked on every tracked run (delete=False, never unlinked)
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified (consolidates the two duplicate reports of this issue): run_study's best-effort _log_trail callback serializes the hash-chained trail to tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile('w', suffix='_provenance.json', delete=False) (pipeline.py:739-740) then calls t.log_artifact(rid, path) (line 744) and never unlinks the file. MLflow copies the file into the artifact store but does not remove the source, so every successful study run with a tracker leaves one orphaned *_provenance.json in TMPDIR; over a long-lived production node these accumulate unbounded. The file also holds provenance payloads at a default-mode temp path. Contrast versioning.py which uses mkstemp + try/finally unlink.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Run run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) N times; ls $TMPDIR/*_provenance.json shows N leaked files. There is no os.unlink in the _log_trail closure or surrounding finally.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Wrap log_artifact in try/finally and os.unlink(path) afterward (MLflow has already copied it), or write into a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() context that auto-cleans. Optionally chmod 0o600 since the file holds provenance payloads.

deduplicated_findings.title: Module-global reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor cannot self-heal if a worker future vanishes — saturation guard can wedge permanently
deduplicated_findings.description: The reasoning seam uses one process-global ThreadPoolExecutor; _submit_bounded increments _inflight before submit and decrements via add_done_callback(_release). Accounting is guarded for KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit and pool-shutdown, but there is no recovery if a worker thread dies abnormally without the future ever completing (a C-level backend crash that takes the worker but not the process, or pool-state corruption): the done-callback never fires, _inflight is never decremented, and after _POOL_WORKERS such events every subsequent call raises RateLimitError('pool saturated') for the rest of the process lifetime. future.result() timeouts are handled (slot intentionally held), but a vanished future is not. Latent availability/leak risk on the exception path; the checkpoint pool by contrast is self-healing.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Monkeypatch _pool.submit to return a Future that never resolves; call _with_timeout _POOL_WORKERS times -> each times out but the futures never complete, so _release never runs; the next _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError unconditionally and stays that way forever.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Add a watchdog/reap step: track each in-flight future with its submission time and, in _submit_bounded's saturation branch, force _release on futures that are done-but-unreleased or have exceeded (per-call timeout + grace) before refusing. Document that wait=False atexit may abandon in-flight backend calls.

deduplicated_findings.title: Unit-level confirmation lacks the directionless-lock refusal its whole-corpus twin enforces (tail chosen post-hoc)
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: confirm_whole_corpus routes the locked direction through _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289), which REFUSES a directionless-but-locked hypothesis (None -> #G-001 HARKing guard) and rejects any tail not in {greater,less}. confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:180-197) only shape-validates a non-None predicted_direction and otherwise lets the caller-supplied direction_supports_claim flag decide; a locked hypothesis with predicted_direction=None passes unit-level confirmation with the polarity effectively chosen post-hoc by the caller — exactly the HARKing case the whole-corpus path refuses. The asymmetry is documented (confirm.py:163-169) but is a cross-mechanism guard-strength divergence.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Lock a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis with predicted_direction=None. confirm_unit_level(..., direction_supports_claim=True) -> supported; flip the flag -> the SAME locked hypothesis -> contradicted. The whole-corpus twin refuses this outright via _resolve_direction.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: If unit-level studies are expected to bind the tail at lock time, add a symmetric refusal: when a unit-level hypothesis is locked, require predicted_direction to be non-None. If the asymmetry must stand, emit a runtime warning when direction_supports_claim is supplied for a directionless-locked hypothesis, mirroring _gate_preregistration's authority=None warning.

deduplicated_findings.title: Confirm-step double-dip leak check (assert_confirm_only) enforced by confirm_unit_level but not by the discovery/pipeline runners
deduplicated_findings.description: DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime guard that the confirm step touched ONLY held-out ids. confirm_unit_level calls it on the caller-supplied evaluated_ids (confirm.py:188). The two composing runners that also confirm — discover_and_confirm (discovery.py) and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block — never call assert_confirm_only; they only verify the held-out partition is non-empty and subset the corpus, then trust the study-supplied confirmer to evaluate within it without checking which ids it actually scored. A confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units is caught by the standalone primitive but NOT by either runner. Firewall-B's leak check exists on one parallel mechanism and is absent on the other two.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Build a confirm_held_out that ignores its held_out arg and evaluates discovery-partition units. confirm_unit_level(hyp, split=split, evaluated_ids=<discovery ids>, ...) raises FirewallViolation 'double-dipping'. The same leak through discover_and_confirm(... confirm=that_callable ...) completes cleanly because the runner never asks the confirmer which ids it evaluated.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Have the discovery runners obtain (or require) the set of ids the confirmer evaluated and pass it through split.assert_confirm_only before accepting the verdict (e.g. have HeldOutConfirmer return evaluated ids alongside the verdict). At minimum document in both runners that the confirm-time double-dip check is delegated to the study, making the asymmetry with confirm_unit_level explicit.

deduplicated_findings.title: Persisted provenance records carry no key/algorithm marker — a keyed (HMAC) trail is indistinguishable from an unkeyed one on re-verify
deduplicated_findings.description: entries_to_records()/to_records() serialize seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash but record nothing about whether the chain was sealed with SHA-256 (unkeyed) or HMAC-SHA256 (keyed). from_records()/verify_records() require the caller to supply the correct secret out-of-band; omitting it on a keyed trail fails with the generic 'content was altered, or the wrong key was used' error, conflating a missing-key config with genuine tampering. Conversely there is no recorded signal that a trail SHOULD have been keyed, so a downstream consumer cannot detect that an audit-grade trail was persisted unkeyed. Weakens the provenance-completeness claim for persisted trails.
deduplicated_findings.poc: trail = ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); trail.record('a','b'); recs = trail.to_records(); from_records(recs) -> raises ProvenanceError as if tampered, because no secret was passed and nothing in recs indicates one is required.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Emit a non-authenticated-but-informative envelope alongside the records (e.g. {'algo':'hmac-sha256'|'sha256','keyed':bool}) so a verifier can fail with a precise 'this trail is keyed; supply the secret' message and a consumer can detect an unkeyed audit trail. Do not include the secret. Optionally fold an algorithm tag into the hashed preimage so it is itself integrity-protected.

deduplicated_findings.title: Operator-username PII not redacted from transcripts when the home-dir name is short (<4 chars) or appears in a dash-delimited project slug
deduplicated_findings.description: _scrub redacts the operator username two ways: the path rule '(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s"']+' (requires a literal '/Users/[REDACTED]') and _USERNAME_RE derived from Path.home().name. The Claude project-dir slug is dash-delimited (e.g. '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc'); the path rule does NOT match it (no slashes), so the only mask inside slugs/git-author strings/prose is _USERNAME_RE — which returns None for any home-dir name shorter than 4 chars (to avoid over-redacting common words). For operators whose login is <4 chars, the username then leaks verbatim into committed transcripts via the slug and prose, defeating the stated #F-024/#G-007 PII-scrubbing goal. The docstring claims operator PII is redacted, so this is an undisclosed completeness gap.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Set HOME so Path.home().name='bob'; create $SRC=.../-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc/<id>.jsonl containing the slug path and 'bob'. Run capture_transcripts — the committed transcript still contains 'bob' and '-Users-[REDACTED]-...' because _USERNAME_RE is None (len<4) and the '/Users/' rule never matches the dash slug.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Always derive the slug form of the home path (replace os.sep with '-') and redact it explicitly regardless of username length; lower/remove the len<4 skip in favor of word-boundary-anchored token redaction. At minimum document that usernames <4 chars are NOT auto-redacted so reviewers scrub before publishing.

deduplicated_findings.title: No concurrency group — superseded PR pushes keep running and audit/lockfile-compile can race
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: ci.yml has no concurrency block, so every push to an open PR spawns the full matrix without cancelling the prior in-flight run. Beyond wasted minutes, two overlapping audit runs on the same branch both run lockfile resolution / 'pip install -e .' and upload identically-named SBOM/license artifacts that can race. On a single dedicated compute node this compounds contention with the reasoning/checkpoint pools.
deduplicated_findings.poc: grep -n concurrency .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. Push twice in quick succession to a PR; both runs execute to completion.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Add a top-level concurrency: { group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: true } so a newer commit cancels the stale run and only one audit/lockfile-compile executes per ref.

deduplicated_findings.title: pip-audit / SBOM each re-fetch the advisory DB and re-resolve — a network blip fails the 'reproducible' audit gate non-reproducibly
deduplicated_findings.description: The audit job invokes pip-audit multiple times (gate at line 131, SBOM generation at line 151) plus a license-gate install, each reaching the OSV vuln DB / PyPI at run time. The stated goal is a reproducible supply-chain gate, but the result still depends on live availability of those services: a transient OSV/PyPI outage fails a commit that passed yesterday — the same non-reproducibility class the version pins were meant to remove. There is no retry, no vuln-DB caching, and the SBOM step re-runs the whole audit instead of reusing the gate's result.
deduplicated_findings.poc: The gate step (line 131) and SBOM step (line 151) both run pip-audit against requirements.lock, each performing its own vulnerability-service lookup; no --cache-dir reuse or retry wrapper. An OSV/PyPI 5xx during any fails the required audit job.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Run pip-audit once with --cache-dir (or a pinned/cached vuln DB), emit both the pass/fail and the CycloneDX SBOM from that single invocation, and wrap network steps in a bounded retry — halving PyPI/OSV calls and removing the duplicate live resolution from the reproducible gate.

deduplicated_findings.title: QdrantVectorStore.query has no k validation and assumes every returned point carries a 'ref' payload (raw KeyError)
deduplicated_findings.description: QdrantVectorStore.query (line 110) is the public VectorStore.query implementation. (1) It does not validate k — a k<=0 is forwarded straight to the backend, unlike upsert() which validates len-equality and batch_size>=1. (2) It reads p.payload['ref'] for every returned point; any point NOT written by this store's upsert() (inserted by another process/tool, or a future schema change) has no 'ref' key and query() raises a bare KeyError rather than a typed store error. The class docstring promises ids round-trip via the 'ref' payload but never defends the read path against foreign points. Both are edge-case gaps at an otherwise-hardened public boundary.
deduplicated_findings.poc: A point upserted by an external writer lacks 'ref'; query() does [(p.payload['ref'], float(p.score)) for p in res.points] -> KeyError 'ref' escapes untyped. Separately store.query(vector, k=0) forwards k=0 with no guard.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Validate k at the top of query ('if k < 1: raise ValueError("k must be >= 1")') to mirror upsert, and read defensively: ref = (p.payload or {}).get('ref'); if ref is None: continue (or raise a typed store error) so a foreign point cannot crash query with a raw KeyError.

deduplicated_findings.title: Import-time int() of ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT (and tracing port) raises an opaque ValueError masquerading as an import failure
deduplicated_findings.description: VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get('ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT', '6333')) executes at module import (vectorstore.py:18); observability/tracing.py does the same for ASSAY_TRACING_PORT (line 30). A non-numeric value raises ValueError during `import`, surfacing as an import-time crash with no context tying it to the offending env var — and for any module importing these transitively it can present as a confusing ImportError-adjacent failure. The codebase elsewhere re-reads and bounds numeric env config at point-of-use with clamping (seam.py, checkpoint.py), so this unbounded import-time parse is a config-error-contract inconsistency. Low because it only triggers on misconfiguration, but the failure mode is opaque.
deduplicated_findings.poc: ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=notaport python -c 'import assay_engine.persistence.vectorstore' -> ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notaport' at import, no env-var context.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Parse these ports lazily inside vector_store_url()/get_qdrant_client() and tracing_endpoint(), or wrap int() in a helper raising a clear configuration error naming the env var. Keeps import side-effect-free and gives an actionable message.

deduplicated_findings.title: redact_creds docstring claims the primary connection string is "always scrubbed precisely"
deduplicated_findings.description: Verified: redact_creds documents 'the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely' (line 140) via layer 1, an exact substring match text.replace(conn_str, sanitized) (line 143). psycopg/libpq error messages do not always echo the DSN verbatim: a password can be URL-decoded, re-quoted, partially rendered, or the host/port reformatted, so the exact-match replace can miss the credential and only the generic best-effort regex backstops catch it. 'always' promises a precision the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Construct a conn_str with a percent-encoded password (e.g. 'a%2Fb') so libpq decodes it before embedding the decoded form in an error string. text.replace(conn_str, ...) does not match the decoded substring; layer 1 misses it and only the regex backstops (best-effort) apply.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely' to '...is scrubbed precisely when the backend echoes it verbatim; the URI-userinfo and DSN-password regex layers backstop other renderings (best-effort).'

deduplicated_findings.title: G-004 in-process unfreeze test is a probabilistic guard and its two assertions use the same set value (redundant coverage)
deduplicated_findings.description: test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (lines 43-48) asserts at the AMBIENT (unpinned) PYTHONHASHSEED. As a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration it is probabilistic — raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} happens to equal the stable order ~30% of seeds, so the buggy code would still pass ~1/3 of runs (the test's own comment at lines 52-53 admits this). Verified secondary defect: line 47 {1,'a',2,'b'} and line 48 {'b',2,'a',1} are the SAME set value with identical per-process iteration order, so line 48 adds zero coverage and gives a false impression of testing two independent orderings. NOTE: the real discriminating guard already exists — test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess (lines 51-69) pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess and fails the buggy code every run — so this is a test-quality/redundancy issue, not a true coverage gap (downgraded from the reported high).
deduplicated_findings.poc: Across 200 PYTHONHASHSEEDs, list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for ~60/200 seeds; on raw-iteration code the in-process assertion passes whenever the ambient seed lands there. And frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) == frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}), so both assertions exercise one ordering.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Treat the in-process assertion as documentation only (the subprocess test is the guard), and replace the duplicate {'b',2,'a',1} on line 48 with a genuinely different input (e.g. frozenset({1,'a',2.5,'b'})) so the two assertions cover distinct cases. Optionally parametrize the in-process check over several pinned-and-differing PYTHONHASHSEEDs run in subprocesses.
